paragraphs.body: An animal that forty percent of households in the United States have. What is so special about dogs that makes many people want one? For some they have a dog to exercise more or to help with loneliness, but why do people with anxiety and depression benefit from owning dogs?

paragraphs.body: In the article, "Dogs and our Emotions" Aaron Llera Ryan Llera insists that dogs understand human emotions without having to say a word through visual cues. Llera is a practicing veterinarian who started writing about what pets might be going through so owners can better understand them. He asserts his point by developing several important ideas. First, Llera observes that dogs can respond to human emotions without fully understanding them. According to Llera, "Dogs, like toddlers, are susceptible to emotional contagion. This means dogs can respond to the emotions of humans without fully understanding what the person is feeling." Llera insists that dogs are similar to a 2-year-old or 3-year-old human when it comes to matters of the mind. Next, he believes dogs' ability to understand human emotions stems from the century-long relationship between humans and canines. Dogs were domesticated thousands of years ago, leaving vast amounts of time to pick up on human tendencies to learn from us. Finally, Llera claims noise helps them pick up on human emotions. The main question being proposed in the article is if dogs truly understand us or just understand the visual and sound cues that humans give off. Research has shown that dogs are more susceptible to emotional cognition. This theory makes sense when looking at more straight forward emotions like anger, sadness, and joy. Llera explains, "Children may not understand why the first baby cried, but they join in the chorus. In a similar way, your emotional state may be contagious to your dog. If you are sad, they are affected by it and come close to nuzzle you. Your dog is comforting you while seeking comfort themselves." His point is that dogs see one person as upset but won't understand why and will look for comfort themselves. However, these theories do not explain how dogs know when you are anxious.

annotations.text: In the article, "Dogs and our Emotions" Aaron Llera Ryan Llera insists that dogs understand human emotions without having to say a word through visual cues.

paragraphs.body: My dogs mean everything to me. Melody was the first dog I ever knew, coming home to her when I was born. I grew up with her, and we went through many moments in my life together. When I was seven, my parents told my sister and me we were moving. I was leaving my friends and family to go to a state I had never been to. I went to a room where I could be alone, but Melody knew something was wrong and followed me. I had never felt more anxious in my life until that point, what if the people were different in this new state? What if they made fun of the toys I played with? Would I ever see my friends and family again? While all these questions went through my mind, Melody was able to understand and be there for me when I did not want anyone else to be.

annotations.text: My dogs mean everything to me. Melody was the first dog I ever knew,
annotations.comment: Nice job shifting to the personal :)

paragraphs.body: As Melody got older, we got two more family dogs, Daisy and Chloe. I spent far more time with them as I was helping to train them. Going through the stress of high school with them, they started to learn when I wasn't feeling myself. Countless nights before finals, SATs, or ACTs, I would be endlessly studying, worrying I would not know anything, thinking I was going to fail or not do well, my dogs would follow me around. The weeks leading up to college were stressful with packing, picking classes, and everything. Every so often, I would hear the footsteps of one of my dogs outside my room, looking to enter. They knew exactly when my mood would change, when to come, and where to find me.

paragraphs.body: After reading Llera's article about how dogs understand human emotions, I could not help but think how my dogs could do the same. Llera states, "We don't have much scientific evidence regarding just how much our dogs understand us; research is ongoing in that arena. Despite the halo of mystery, we do know that part of a dog's ability to understand human emotions stems from the centuries-long relationship between humans and canines." Lleras's point is that while no one fully knows why dogs can understand human emotions as they do, part of the reason is their relationships with humans. Every person in this world expresses emotions differently, so how would this factor in how dogs can determine how you feel? Llera brings up another point about dogs using scent to sniff out sweat samples of humans. According to Llera, "When exposed to the fear sweat sample, the dogs' heart rates went up, and they sought comfort from their owners, ignoring the stranger. When exposed to the happy sample, the dogs were more relaxed and less wary of the stranger." Basically, Llera is saying that dogs can smell how you are feeling, as sweat glands smell differently based on your emotions. If this statement is true, do dogs care about you, or do they come over because you smell different?

annotations.text: If this statement is true, do dogs care about you, or do they come over because you smell different?

paragraphs.body: For as long as I can remember, I have struggled with anxiety, and my dogs have always been there to support me. Thousands of nights before tests, big games, and many other events were anxious for me, and my dog sat by me when I needed it. Not only do they come to comfort me when I am anxious, but they are sad and excited. Each time, they show emotion, too. They jump on me when excited, cry to be pet when sad, and cuddle next to me when nervous. The big question is how they can do this. Are dogs like two- to three-year-old children and pick up on their emotions? Are humans just a scent, a visual cue, or a sound to them? Despite not understanding the scientific reasoning behind why dogs are extremely comforting, they will always be a "man's best friend."

annotations.text: my dogs have always been there to support me.
Despite not understanding the scientific reasoning behind why dogs are extremely comforting, they will always be a "man's best friend."
